body_txt: One of the key questions in detective stories is: who has a motive? Usually that only serves to narrow things down, as there are two or three people with a plausible motive, and even then a whole other person can turn out to be the actual killer. Sometimes the opposite happens: every single person in the general vicinity has a reason to want the victim dead. Not only was the victim a horrible human being whom everybody hated on general principle , but the victim also had specifically wronged all the other characters. The killer could have been anyone . This is a common reason for a Chekhov's Party , because it'll usually turn out a lot of important suspects were collected together for some reason. Sometimes everyone's a suspect because Everybody Did It . Long story short, it's better to have lots and lots of suspects, because that is part of what makes it a mystery.
